name: Kit: Peasant Hero
raw_text:
Peasant Hero 
Description: The Peasant 
Hero Is the "local boy done 
good," the home-town warrior 
who fights and adventures to 
the deUght of the people In his 
home area. The Peasant Hero Is 
the most common sort of fighter 
found wandering the land and 
advemurlng: every village has 
one or has had one within living 
memory. 
There are no ability-score reo 
quirements to be a Peasant 
Hero, 
Role: In the campaign, the 
Peasant Hero is the fellow who 
won '[ forgel that his roots are In 
the country and in the soil. He 
can be a rebel against the crown 
in lands where the peasants are 
especially oppressed: he can be 
the farm boy who becomes a 
mighty generaJ: he can be the 
noble's child (secretly raised by 
peasants) who grows up to fulflll 
an ancient prophecy: but in 
every case, he remembers his 
origins and strives to make 
things better for his family and 
home community, 
Secondary Skills: The player 
may choose his character's secondary skill. 
Weapon Proriciencies: The 
player may choose his charac· 
ter's weapon proficlencies, but 
may not choose any that the OM 
feels would be unusual for his 
19 
campalgn·world's 
peasants, 
Short sword, spear, bow, footman's weapons and the like are 
all very appropriate; horse· 
man's weapons. exotic polearrns, lances. long swords, 
tridents and the like are not. 
This is only a restriction when 
the character Is first created: af· 
terwards, of course. he can I~arn 
any weapon he receives training 
with. 
Nonweapon Proriciencies: 
Bonus Proficlencies: Agrlcul· 
ture or Fishing (player choice), 
Weather Sense or Animal Lore 
(player choice). Recommended: 
Any of the General proficien· 
cles. 
Equipment: The Peasant 
Hero may spend his starting 
gold any way he sees fit, but 
may have no more than 3 gp left 
when he begins play. 
Special Benefits: No matter 
what he's done or what a nyone 
thinks of him, the Peasant Hero 
always has shelter and often has 
other help when he's in his own 
community. Unless It is known 
that the Peasant Hero has hurt 
people from his own communtty, he'll always find people to 
put him up, hide him and companions from the law, supply 
them with food and drink and 
what little weaponry can be 
scraped together (usually dag· 
gers), and even provide them 
with helpers-earnest O·level 
youths who want to grow up to 
be like their hero. 
Special Hindrances: Since 
the Peasant Hero Is looked upon 
as a patron and hero by the peo. 
pIe from his home. they will frequently come to him for help. 
Whenever the viJlage Is losing 
people to nocturnal predators. 
whenever a v1llage overlord 
turns out to be a dangerous ty· 
rant, whenever a local citizen Is 
Jailed and tried for something he 
didn't do. the citizens turn to 
the Peasant Hero for help. And If 
he turns them away. he loses 
their respect and earns a - 2 re-

stl/ Warrior Kits 
, 
action from all aflhe peasants in 
the land until he Is once again in 
his home communlty's good 
graces. 
Wealth Options: The Peasant 
Hero gets the standard 5d4xlO 
gp starting money. 
Races: The Peasant Hero Is a 
dIstinctly human sort of character: It's also appropriate to halflings. and to half-elves living 
among humans. But no other 
demihumans should have Peasant Hero characters unless the 
DM decides thal their cultures 
are very much like rural human 
society. 
Ph'ate/Outlaw 
Description: This character 
Is the heroic scomaw. {he warrior who defies the laws and 
rulers of the land and steers his 
own course. Usually In the company of other pirates or outlaws. 
h e nghLS the minions of l.he 
rulers he defies. and comes to be 
regarded as a hero by others 
who suffer at those rulers' 
hands. The Pirate. of course. is 
the adventurer of the high seas. 
who makes his living raiding 
other ships and seacoast communities: the Outlaw makes his 
home In the wilderness (often 
deep forest) and preys on the 
traffic moving through that wilderness. 
There are no special abilityscore requirements to be a PIrate or Outlaw. 
Role: In a campaign. the pi· 
rate or outlaw can belong to one 
ofrwo orientations. Either he's a 
"good guy." and It Is the law and 
the rulers who are evil, or he is a 
"bad guy" and simply takes 
what he wants from those who 
have it. The player. therefore, 
gelS to decide on his character's 
aUgnment and (mls}dceds. 
Note. though. that good guy 
pirates and outlaws tend to live 
by a 
very strict code of 
conduct- for example, the classic cinema code of Robin Hood 
and his Merry Men, where the 
outlaws robbed from the rich 
and gave to the poor. and protected the defenseless with 
more honor and zeal than England's supposedly Noble Warriors did. 
Naturally, If a player chooses 
to bea good guy in a company of 
bad guys. or vice-versa, when 
his companions find out his true 
colors. they'll probably try to 
kill hIm or to turn him in to the 
law (or the reward on his head. 
Secondary Skills: If the character is a Pirate, roll d 100 for his 
Secondary Skill. On a 0 1-70, his 
Secondary Skill Is Sailor: on a 
7 1-80. It's Shipwright: on 8 1·00. 
It's Navigator. If the character is 
an Outlaw. the character may 
choose 
between 
Bowyerl 
Fletcher, Foresier. Hunter. and 
Trapper/Furrier. 
Weapon Proficiencies: If the 
character Is a Pirate. he must 
take the following profieieneles: 
Cutlass· . and Belaying Pin - or 
Gaff/Hook - (player ehoicel. If 
the character is an Outlaw. he 
can take any weapon proficJencles he chooses ... but the DM. 
if he's created this campaign SO 
that the outlaws have a special 
motif weapon (such as Robin 
Hood's Merr), Men and their 
longbows} may insist that all 
Outlaw characters take a specific weapon proficiency. Rec· 
ommended to classic Merry 
Man-type outlaws arc longbow, 
long sword and quarlerstafr. 
(The " . " symbol Indlcates a 
new weapon found In the Equipment chapter.)

(General) Rope Use, Seaman· 
s hip. Pirate's Recommended

ming, Weather Sense, (Warrior) 
Navigation. (Priest. double slots 
unless Paladin) Engineering (for 
shipbuilding). Reading/Writing 
(for mapmaking), (Rogue, dou· 
ble slots) Appraising. Set Snarcs 
(In association with Rope Use 
30 
skill). Tightrope Walking, TUm· 
bling, (Wizard, double slots unless Ranger) Engineering (for 
shipbuilding). ReadlnglWnting 
(for mapmaking). Outlaw's Bonus Profielencles: Direction 
Sense, Flre·Bulldlng. Outlaw's 
Recommended Proficiencles: 
(General) Riding (Land·Based). 
(Warrior) Animal Lore, Bowyerl 
Fletcher, Endurance, Hunting, 
Running. Set Snares. Survival. 
Tracking, (Prlese double slots 
unless Paladin) Healing. Herbalism. Local History. (Rogue. dou· 
ble slots) Disguise. Special Note: 
Your OM may bea fan of the very 
acrobatic pirate or outlaw movies of the past. and prefer that 
Thmbling be one of your Bonus 
Proficiencies Instead of one of 
the ones listed: check with him 
La sec If this Is so. 
Equipment: Pirates and Out· 
laws come from widely diverse 
backgrounds. so there's no real 
restriction on what t.hey can buy 
with their starting money. How· 
ever, It would be foollsh for either 
type of character to buy metal ar· 
mor of any kJnd (banded, brlgandine. bronze plale. chain , field 
plate, full plate. plate mall. and 
ring maill. Pirates wearing such 
annal' In naval combat willlnevl· 
tably fall overboard and sink 
(they can't swim with such stuff 
on): if they're lucky enough to 
get it off so they can swim , 
they've lost the annor. Outlaws 
living out In the wild have their 
belongings exposed to the ele· 
ments. and metal annol' qUickly 
corrodes. Therefore. It's up to the 
DM to keep things in balance. lfa 
Pirate or Outlaw buys metal armor and keeps It stowed away 
for special occasions (major land 
engagements, climactic battles, 
etc.), that's fine. But if they wear 
such stuff all the time. the DM 
should continually take It away 
from them through accidents. 
rust and corrosion, etc.
